Edition 03 · 2026
01 · Discover

Competitor Analysis

1. Overview and objectives

This analysis maps where Maxxim competes so the Discover phase can take a defensible position on the core question: what Maxxim is actually becoming. The goal is not to name a single rival to beat. It is to show that Maxxim competes by category on two layers at once, and that no category in either layer combines the three things Maxxim pairs together: a proven method, AI speed, and an accountable human at every gate.

The two layers are distinct because Maxxim serves two customers. The partner layer is the primary customer and the scale mechanism: the trusted operator who licenses or refers. The end-client layer is the SME commercialised through that partner. Each layer has its own substitutes, so each is analysed separately and then drawn together.

Since the 27 June version, two things have sharpened this picture. First, the product mechanics are now known in detail: three named AI specialists (Max, Sal, Pip), the Design Studio and Social Studio, mechanical gating, an Astro-plus-Cloudflare hosting stack, a swappable best-tool-for-each-job engine, and a client portal with a private assistant called Ari. These are product truth, and they are the concrete evidence that Maxxim is not just tooling. Second, Jewell is now a live, in-market partner running on Maxxim, which is the first real proof that the partner model works and the clearest articulation of the human-machine seam. Both are used below as differentiation evidence.

What this document is built to inform: positioning, the category lead word (currently OPEN), the front-door product (currently OPEN), and the proof path. It does not settle any of those. It supplies the competitive evidence that the strategy work will stand on.

Source note. Claims here are labelled [Discovery] when drawn from the Master Briefing v2.1 and Discovery Dataset, [Product] when drawn from the 29 June product guide, [Jewell] when drawn from the live Jewell partner site, [Category research] when describing how a competitor category generally operates, and [Assumption] when inferred and pending confirmation. Per the engagement guardrails, no named-competitor specifics are fabricated and no partner economics are stated as locked.

2. Competitor identification

Competition is by category, not a single named rival. [Discovery, Q21] Five categories carry the load across the two layers. Named real-world brands exist inside each category, but the strategic unit is the category, so these are profiled as archetypes.

End-client layer

  • A. Small marketing agencies. Local or boutique shops selling marketing as a service to SMEs. Private, owner-operated, founder-capped. [Discovery, Q21]
  • B. Small web developers. Freelancers and small studios building sites for tradies and small businesses. Private, often single-operator. [Discovery, Q21]
  • C. DIY plus general AI tools. The owner doing it themselves on a site builder such as Squarespace, plus general AI assistants for copy and images. Self-serve SaaS and horizontal AI products. [Discovery, Q21]

Partner layer

  • D. White-label and agency-enablement platforms. "Agency-in-a-box" products that give an operator tooling, dashboards, and reseller mechanics to run client work under their own brand. Private SaaS, typically venture or bootstrap funded. [Discovery, Q21; partner positioning inferred from v2.1]
  • E. Self-assembled AI toolchains. Operators stitching their own stack together from horizontal AI tools, builders, and freelance labour. Not a vendor at all; a build-your-own substitute. [Discovery, Q21]

Indirect substitutes across both layers: hiring an in-house marketer, an operator building a bespoke process, generic SaaS website builders, and doing nothing. [Discovery, Q26]

Positioning frame. Maxxim is the trustworthy middle between two failure modes the buyer already knows: slow, expensive, opaque legacy delivery on one side (Categories A and B), and fast but unaccountable pure-AI or DIY on the other (Categories C and E). Categories D and E are the partner-layer version of the same split: reach or control, but no method and no quality bar. The rest of this document tests that frame against how each category actually operates.

3. Brand positioning and messaging

How each category positions itself, and the tone it tends to carry. [Category research]

CategoryTypical positioningImplied promiseTone
A. Small marketing agencies"Your local marketing partner who gets results."Human expertise and a named person who cares.Relationship-led, reassuring, bespoke.
B. Small web developers"A professional website for your business, fast."A site that looks the part without DIY effort.Practical, craft-focused, project-based.
C. DIY plus AI tools"Build it yourself in an afternoon."Speed, control, and very low cost.Empowering, frictionless, self-serve.
D. Enablement platforms"Run your own agency with our platform."Reach, margin, and reseller scale.Growth-hungry, feature-rich, scale-led.
E. Self-assembled toolchainsNo external message; this is the operator's own decision to build.Full control and no licence fee.Pragmatic, self-reliant.

Maxxim's stated position is the trustworthy middle: a proven method plus AI speed plus a human partner who owns the relationship and signs off at every gate. Finished outcomes, owned assets, named accountability. [Discovery, Q24, Q25]

What the live Jewell partner site now shows. Jewell articulates the same seam in market as a partner running on Maxxim: "Strategy first. AI enabled. Human led. Built to move what matters." Jewell owns the human 20 percent, which is trust, taste and judgement, and sets the standard the machine must meet; Maxxim is the machine 80 percent, the engine behind the partner, not the name on the door. [Jewell] This matters competitively because it is the working model of Maxxim's positioning, not a slide. Note that Jewell's own front-door packaging (a Growth Diagnostic, then a Fractional CMO engine) is Jewell's packaging, not Maxxim's; the fractional CMO model and the front-door product remain OPEN for Maxxim and are not adopted here as settled Maxxim facts. [Discovery, open items; Jewell] Maxxim's own category lead word is likewise OPEN and must not be invented. [Discovery, open items]

4. Product and service comparison

What each category actually delivers, and how. [Category research]

CategoryCore offerDelivery modelSpeedPricing pattern
A. Small agenciesStrategy plus execution as a service.Human-delivered, often retainer.Weeks to months.Monthly retainer or project fee.
B. Small web developersA built website, sometimes plus basics.Human-delivered, project-based.Days to weeks.One-off build fee, optional hosting.
C. DIY plus AI toolsTemplates plus self-generated content.Self-serve software.Hours, but owner does the work.Low monthly subscription.
D. Enablement platformsTooling and reseller mechanics for the operator.SaaS the partner operates.Depends on the operator.Platform subscription, often per-seat or per-client.
E. Self-assembled toolchainsWhatever the operator wires together.Operator-built and operator-run.Variable; setup cost is high.Sum of tool subscriptions plus the operator's time.
MaxximFull gated 3D engagement: strategy plus launched, owned assets, then a Deepen loop.AI runs the repeatable 80 percent through Max, Sal and Pip; the human partner owns the 20 percent and signs off at each gate.Days not months for the end-client outcome.Sprint $4,950 credited; core $25K to $250K (recent run ~$80K); platform floor plus clip. Partner economics illustrative until pilots. [Discovery, Q9, Q10; OPEN]

The product stack, now known, is the differentiation evidence. [Product] Where competitors offer a person, a template, or a bag of tools, Maxxim offers an assembled engine with the human placed exactly where quality is decided:

  • Three named specialists, each a process. Max does the words (strategy, plans, copy, briefs) inside Claude Cowork; Sal makes the pixels (logos, brand imagery, collateral, video) in the Design Studio; Pip drafts and, on approval, publishes posts in the Social Studio. This is a division of labour a DIY buyer or a solo developer cannot replicate, and a coherence an operator's self-assembled toolchain rarely achieves.
  • Gating is real and mechanical, not a promise. Creative work unlocks only as the briefs it depends on exist: a logo needs the Logo Brief, brand imagery needs the Brand Guidelines, collateral needs the guidelines plus a locked logo, the Social Studio unlocks only once the Social Strategy exists. The quality bar is enforced by the system, not left to the operator's discipline. This is the direct counter to "tooling with no method" (Categories D and E).
  • A swappable, best-tool-for-each-job engine. Photographic raster from one model, flat and vector from another, video from another, premium hero imagery from another, publishing and email through best-in-class services. Only the thin layer (Max plus the engine) is built in-house; everything else is deliberately leaned on. A competitor betting on a single model, or an operator locked into one tool, cannot match the range without matching the assembly.
  • Owned, hosted outcomes. Client websites are Astro on Cloudflare Pages with a free subdomain and custom domains, images served from a dedicated asset host, embedded apps such as a contact form. The client owns the result outright. Sites are locked by default and can be unlocked to hand-editable HTML as a one-way door, which is a real ownership guarantee, not a hostage arrangement.
  • A read-only client portal with Ari. Clients can be given a cut-down portal with Files, a private business assistant called Ari, and Help. This is a retention and trust surface that agencies and builders rarely offer and DIY cannot.

Strategic implication. Categories A and B sell human delivery but cannot deliver at AI speed or repeatably at scale. Category C delivers speed but offloads the work and the judgement onto the owner. Categories D and E hand over tooling but no method and no quality bar. Maxxim is the only model in the set that pairs a finished, owned outcome with both speed and a named human standing behind it, and the product stack above is why that pairing is credible rather than aspirational. [Discovery, Q22, Q23; Product]

5. Market strategy

Who each category targets and how it goes to market. [Category research, with Maxxim from Discovery]

  • A. Small agencies. Target local SMEs through referral, networking, and reputation. Growth is capped by the founder's hours, which is the same constraint Maxxim exists to remove for Jewell. [Discovery, Q2]
  • B. Small web developers. Target small businesses needing a site, won on price and portfolio. Project-by-project, little recurring revenue.
  • C. DIY plus AI tools. Mass self-serve acquisition through search, app stores, and product-led funnels. Volume over relationship.
  • D. Enablement platforms. Target operators and aspiring agency owners with a reseller pitch. The watch-out: these platforms can move upmarket into method, which is a named market threat. [Discovery, Q28]
  • E. Self-assembled toolchains. No go-to-market; this is the default an operator falls into without a better option. Maxxim's partner pitch competes directly against "I will just build my own."
  • Maxxim. Partner-first. Win trusted operators through two doors, license to deliver or refer for a finder's fee, then serve end clients through them. One named partner owns each client relationship, which is the single non-negotiable public rule of the model. [Discovery, Q11; Jewell] The live Jewell flow shows the intended shape: the client enters through the partner, the Discover phase is always human, and Design and Deploy hand off to Maxxim at scale. [Jewell] Near-term go-to-market is showcase-driven: the 3 July partner discovery session, then a founding cohort of 5 to 8 partners across July to September. [Discovery, Q29; the showcase promise itself is OPEN]

6. Marketing presence

Where each category is visible. [Category research]

CategoryPrimary channelsRelative spend and sophistication
A. Small agenciesLocal SEO, referral, social proof, networking.Low to moderate; relationship-driven.
B. Small web developersPortfolio sites, marketplaces, word of mouth.Low; project-led.
C. DIY plus AI toolsPaid search, content, app stores, product-led growth.High; well-funded horizontal players.
D. Enablement platformsB2B content, webinars, affiliate and reseller programmes.Moderate to high; channel-led.
E. Self-assembled toolchainsNot applicable.Not applicable.
MaxximFounder network and partner referral today; maxxim.ai as the proof surface; a live partner in market (Jewell) demonstrating the model; the 3 July showcase as the next deliberate channel.Early; Maxxim-brand marketing is nascent while the method itself has a multi-year track record through Jewell. [Discovery, Q19, Q30; Jewell]

Strategic implication. Maxxim cannot out-spend Category C and does not need to. Its presence strategy is proof-led and partner-led: the live site, a live partner running the model in market, and named, cleared results do the persuading, not ad volume. Jewell's presence is a genuine asset here because it shows a 35-year operator standing behind the method, but Jewell's proof library lives under Jewell's banner and does not become Maxxim's own public proof without being attributed as Jewell's. This raises, rather than lowers, the value of closing Maxxim's own cleared-proof gap. [Discovery, Q20; Jewell; proof and clearance]

7. Customer experience and reputation

No third-party review or NPS data was supplied for any category, so this section is qualitative and category-level. [Assumption, grounded in Discovery, Q12, Q15]

  • A. Small agencies. Valued for the human relationship and accountability. Common friction: slow, expensive, opaque, and dependent on one or two people. [Discovery, Q23]
  • B. Small web developers. Valued for a tangible deliverable. Common friction: the site is built and then handed over with no strategy and no ongoing growth.
  • C. DIY plus AI tools. Valued for speed and control. Common friction: overwhelming, requires prompting skill, and feels unaccountable, which is the AI-fatigue counter-trend Maxxim is built to exploit. [Discovery, Q23, Q27]
  • D. Enablement platforms. Valued for reach and margin. Common friction: tooling without a method or a quality bar, so output quality is uneven and rests entirely on the operator. [Discovery, Q23]
  • E. Self-assembled toolchains. Valued for control and no licence fee. Common friction: high setup cost, no repeatability, and no one to vouch for the result.
  • Maxxim. The proof position is strong but narrow. Pottsville Acupuncture is the only cleared public proof: a launched, full 3D run where the client owns the site outright. Other runs are internal or clearance-restricted and must not be shown publicly. The product now adds an experience advantage that competitors rarely match: a read-only client portal with Ari and Files, and outcomes the client owns outright and can unlock to hand-editable HTML. [Product] Sourcing additional cleared, named results remains the single highest-value reputation action. [Discovery, Q20; proof and clearance]

8. Strengths and weaknesses

Side-by-side across all five categories and Maxxim. [Discovery, Q22, Q23, Q24; Category research; Product]

FactorMaxximA. Small agenciesB. Web developersC. DIY plus AID. Enablement platformsE. Self-assembled toolchains
Strengths Proven gated method; AI speed via Max, Sal and Pip; mechanical gating that enforces the quality bar; swappable best-tool engine; accountable human at every gate; finished, owned, hosted outcomes; client portal with Ari; recurring Deepen loop; a live partner (Jewell) already running the model. Human expertise; accountability; local trust and relationship. Tangible deliverable; low cost; fast for a single site. Cheapest; instant; full owner control. Reach; reseller margin; ready tooling and dashboards. No licence fee; full control of the stack.
Weaknesses Brand-new and largely unproven publicly; one cleared case study; partner economics not yet locked; front-door product and category lead word still OPEN; depends on partner quality and the trust gap ("can AI do this to a standard I can put my name on?"). Slow; expensive; opaque; founder-capped; rarely AI-fast. No strategy; no ongoing growth; not repeatable at scale. Offloads the work and judgement onto the owner; unaccountable; quality varies. Tooling without a method or quality bar; output quality rests on the operator. High setup cost; no repeatability; no method; no one to vouch for it.

Honest read on Maxxim's weakness. The standout vulnerability is the proof gap and the trust gap, not the offer. The offer is now clearly differentiated by a real product stack and a live partner in market; the public evidence base is still thin, with one cleared case study of Maxxim's own. Every category competitor has more market history. This makes cleared proof and a crisp front-door product the two things that most reduce competitive risk. [Discovery, Q15, Q20; both the front-door product and the category word are OPEN]

9. Key differentiators

What Maxxim can claim that no single category in the set can. [Discovery, Q4, Q24, Q25; Product; Jewell]

  • A proven, gated method, enforced by the product, not just tooling. The 3D Process has been run with real clients for years through Jewell, and the platform now enforces the method mechanically: creative work unlocks only as its briefs exist and a logo is locked. Categories D and E offer tools with no method; Maxxim productises a method that already works and builds the gates into the machine. [Product]
  • AI speed with a human accountable at every gate. Days not months, but with a named person who signs off. The explicit bet is "good, fast, cheap, pick three," achieved by keeping the human exactly where quality is decided while AI carries the repeatable volume. Category A has the human without the speed; Category C has the speed without the human. Maxxim is the only model pairing both. [Product]
  • An assembled specialist engine, not one tool. Max for words, Sal for pixels, Pip for social, each a process, running best-in-class models chosen per job. An operator can buy any one tool; none of the competitor categories assembles the whole engine with a method around it. [Product]
  • Finished outcomes the client owns and can walk away with. Strategy plus launched, hosted assets the client owns outright, with sites unlockable to hand-editable HTML. Evidenced by Pottsville. Agencies often hold the keys; builders hand over a shell; DIY leaves the owner to finish it. [Product; Discovery, Q20]
  • A licensable engine with the human seam already proven in market. Maxxim lets a trusted operator deliver agency-grade outcomes repeatably under their own relationship, and Jewell is the live proof of that seam: the partner owns trust, taste and judgement, Maxxim is the engine behind the partner, not the name on the door. This is the answer to "I will just build my own" (Category E) and to "tooling with no method" (Category D). [Jewell]
  • A compounding Deepen loop. The engagement does not end at launch; it improves on a loop, which is the recurring-revenue line agencies rarely systematise and tools do not offer at all. [Product]

Constraint. These are positioning differentiators, not settled claims. The category lead word, the front-door product, the fractional CMO model, and end-client visibility remain OPEN and must be resolved by the strategy work before they drive public copy. Jewell's own packaging illustrates the method but is Jewell's, not Maxxim's. [Discovery, open items; Jewell]

10. Critical success factors

What determines who wins in this space, and how Maxxim is placed on each. [Discovery, Q15, Q20, Q27, Q28; Product]

  • Trust in AI-assisted output. The central buying objection is "can AI do this to a standard I can put my name on?" Whoever closes the trust gap wins. Maxxim's human-at-every-gate model, now mechanically enforced by gating and sign-off, is built for exactly this, bu
Source capture ends here — full document held in the Maxxim engine
